subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f$ and $g$ be functions satisfying $f(x+y)=f(x) f(y), f(1)=7$ and $g(x+y)=g(x y), g(1)=1$, for all $x, y \in \mathbf{N}$. If $\sum_{x=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left(\frac{f(x)}{\mathrm{g}(x)}\right)=19607$, then n is equal to:
question: Let $f$ and $g$ be functions satisfying $f(x+y)=f(x) f(y), f(1)=7$ and $g(x+y)=g(x y), g(1)=1$, for all $x, y \in \mathbf{N}$. If $\sum_{x=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left(\frac{f(x)}{\mathrm{g}(x)}\right)=19607$, then n is equal to:
solution:
$f(x+y)=f(x)f(y)$ with $f(1)=7$ gives $f(x)=7^x$.
For $g$: put $y=1$ in $g(x+y)=g(xy)$ to get $g(x+1)=g(x)$.
So $g(x)=g(1)=1$ for all $x \in \mathbb{N}$.
$\sum_{x=1}^{n}\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}=\sum_{x=1}^{n}7^x=\frac{7(7^n-1)}{6}=19607$
$7^{n+1}-7=117642$
$ \Rightarrow 7^{n+1}=117649=7^6$
$n+1=6 \Rightarrow n=5$

question: The graph of the function $y=f(x)$ is symmetrical about the line $x=2$, then

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

f(n)+1nf(n+1)=1,∀ n∈{1,2,3}

⇒nf(n)+f(n+1)=n

At n=1,

f(1)+f(2)=1    ....(1)

At n=2,

2f(2)+f(3)=2    ....(2)

At n=3,

3f(3)+f(4)=3      ....(3)

Put the value of f(2) from equation (1) in equation (2),

2(1-f(1))+f(3)=2

⇒f(3)=2f(1)       ....(4)

Put the value of f(3) from equation (4) in equation (3),

3(2f(1))+f(4)=3

⇒f(4)=3-6f(1)    

∵ f:{1,2,3,4}→{a∈ℤ:|a|≤8}

⇒-8≤f(4)≤8

⇒-8≤3-6f(1)≤8

⇒-11≤-6f(1)≤5

⇒-56≤f(1)≤116

⇒f(1)=0, 1

Case I: f(1)=0

⇒f(2)=1, f(3)=0, f(4)=3

Case II: f(1)=1

⇒f(2)=0, f(3)=2, f(4)=-3

Therefore, two such functions are possible.

subject: Mathematics
q: The sum of all the elements in the range of $f(x)=\operatorname{Sgn}(\sin x)+\operatorname{Sgn}(\cos x)+\operatorname{Sgn}(\tan x)+\operatorname{Sgn}(\cot x)$, $x \neq \frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{2}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathbf{Z}$, where $\operatorname{Sgn}(\mathrm{t})=\left\{\begin{aligned} 1, & \text { if } \mathrm{t}>0 \\ -1, & \text { if } \mathrm{t}<0\end{aligned}\right.$, is :
question: The sum of all the elements in the range of $f(x)=\operatorname{Sgn}(\sin x)+\operatorname{Sgn}(\cos x)+\operatorname{Sgn}(\tan x)+\operatorname{Sgn}(\cot x)$, $x \neq \frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{2}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathbf{Z}$, where $\operatorname{Sgn}(\mathrm{t})=\left\{\begin{aligned} 1, & \text { if } \mathrm{t}>0 \\ -1, & \text { if } \mathrm{t}<0\end{aligned}\right.$, is :
solution:
$x \in (0, \pi/2) \Rightarrow y = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4$
$x \in (\pi/2, \pi) \Rightarrow y = 1 - 1 - 1 - 1 = -2$
$x \in (\pi, 3\pi/2) \Rightarrow y = -1 - 1 + 1 + 1 = 0$
$x \in (3\pi/2, 2\pi) \Rightarrow y = -1 + 1 - 1 - 1 = -2$
$\therefore$ Range of y is $\{-2, 0, 4\}$
Required sum $= -2 + 0 + 4 = 2$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of distinct real roots of the equation $|x||x+2|-5|x+1|-1=0$ is_______
question: The number of distinct real roots of the equation $|x||x+2|-5|x+1|-1=0$ is_______
solution: Case-1 $\begin{aligned} & x \geq 0 \\ & x^2+2 x-5 x-5-1=0 \\ & x^2-3 x-6=0 \\ & x=\frac{3 \pm \sqrt{9+24}}{2}=\frac{3 \pm \sqrt{33}}{2} \end{aligned}$ One positive root Case-2 $\begin{aligned} & -1 \leq x < 0 \\ & -x^2-2 x-5 x-5-1=0 \\ & x^2+7 x+6=0 \\ & (x+6)(x+1)=0 \\ & x=-1 \end{aligned}$ one root in range Case-3 $\begin{aligned} & -2 \leq x < -1 \\ & x^2-2 x+5 x+5-1=0 \\ & x^2-3 x-4=0 \\ & (x-4)(x+1)=0 \end{aligned}$ No root in range Case-4 $\begin{aligned} & x < -2 \\ & x^2+7 x+4=0 \\ & x=\frac{-7 \pm \sqrt{49-16}}{2}=\frac{7 \pm \sqrt{33}}{2} \end{aligned}$ one root in range Total number of distinct roots are 3

q: If the domain of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>11</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>]</mo></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If the domain of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>11</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>]</mo></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given that fx=log4x2+11x+6+sin-14x+3+cos-110x+63

We know that the domain of Ax+Bx+Cx is DA∩DB∩DC.

Let us find the domain of log4x2+11x+6.

We know that domain of logfx is fx>0.

⇒4x2+11x+6>0

⇒4x2+8x+3x+6>0

⇒4x+3x+2>0

⇒x∈-∞,-2∪-34,∞

Domain of log4x2+11x+9 is x∈-∞,-2∪-34,∞.

Let us find the domain of sin-14x+3

⇒-1≤4x+3≤1

⇒-1≤x≤-12

⇒x∈-1,-12

Let us find the domain of cos-110x+63.

⇒-1≤10x+63≤1

⇒-3≤10x+6≤3

⇒-910≤x≤-310.

Now the common domain is -34≤x≤-12.

⇒x∈-34,-12

But given that the domain of fx is x∈α,β.

⇒α=-34, β=-12.

⇒36α+β=36-34+-12

⇒36α+β=36×-54

⇒36α+β=45

Therefore, the required answer is 45.

solution:
Domain of fogx=sin-1gx

⇒g(x)≤1,g2=37

x2-x-22x2-x-6≤1

x+1x-22x+3x-2≤1

x+12x+3≤1 and x+12x+3≥-1

x+1-2x-32x+3≤0 and x+1+2x+32x+3≥0

solution:
Given,

fx=sin-1x2-3x+2x2+2x+7

We know that domain of sin-1fx is -1≤fx≤1

So, x2-3x+2x2+2x+7≥-1 and x2-3x+2x2+2x+7≤1

Now solving x2-3x+2x2+2x+7≥-1

Since denominator x2+2x+7 is always positive,

So, x2-3x+2≥-x2+2x+7

⇒2x2-x+9≥0

⇒x∈R .....1

Now solving x2-3x+2x2+2x+7≤1

⇒x2-3x+2≤x2+2x+7

⇒5x≥-5

⇒x≥-1 .....2

So from equation  1 & 2 we get domain x∈[-1,∞)

q: The number of bijective function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>⋯</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>99</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>⋯</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>3</mn></mfenced><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>5</mn></mfenced><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>7</mn></mfenced><mo>⋯</mo><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>99</mn></mfenced></math> is
question: The number of bijective function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>⋯</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>99</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>⋯</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>3</mn></mfenced><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>5</mn></mfenced><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>7</mn></mfenced><mo>⋯</mo><mo>></mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>99</mn></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Given, f1,3,5,7,⋯,99→2,4,6,8,⋯,100

The number of elements in domain and codomain is 99.

Now, let us assume f1=100

Now as per diagram we have only 1 way for arranging f3>f5>f7⋯>f99,

Similarly if we choose f1=98 then again we have only 1 way for arranging  f3>f5>f7⋯>f99,

So we can see the arrangement f3>f5>f7⋯>f99 depends upon f1,

So number of ways for choosing f1 is C150

q: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3,4\}$ and $\mathrm{B}=\{1,4,9,16\}$. Then the number of many-one functions $f: \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ such that $1 \in f(\mathrm{~A})$ is equal to :
question: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3,4\}$ and $\mathrm{B}=\{1,4,9,16\}$. Then the number of many-one functions $f: \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ such that $1 \in f(\mathrm{~A})$ is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& A=\{1,2,3,4\} \\
& B=\{1,4,9,16\}
\end{aligned}$

Total number of functions $=4^4$
Total number of one-one functions $=4!$
Total number of many one functions $=4^4-4!=232$
Total number of many-one functions in which $1 \notin f(A)=3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3=81$
$\therefore$ Total number of many one functions $1 \notin f(A)$
$\begin{aligned}
& =232-81 \\
& =151
\end{aligned}$

question: Let f:R→R and g:R→R be defined as fx=logex,x>0e−x,x≤0 and gx=x,x≥0ex,x<0. Then, gof:R→R is:
solution:

⇒gfx=fx,fx≥0efx,fx<0

⇒gfx=e−x,−∞,0elogx,0,1logx,1,∞
